note: UPSC key terms glossary. Matched case-insensitively against headline titles and excerpts.

terms.def: Comptroller and Auditor General — Constitutional authority (Article 148) that audits Union and state government accounts and reports to Parliament/Legislature.
Chief Election Commissioner — Head of the Election Commission of India; supervises all elections to Parliament and state legislatures.
Constitutional body (Article 324) responsible for administering elections to Parliament, state legislatures, and offices of President and Vice-President.
National Human Rights Commission — Statutory body (Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993) that investigates violations of human rights and recommends remedies.
National Commission for Women — Statutory body established 1992 to review constitutional safeguards for women and recommend remedial legislative measures.
Union Public Service Commission — Constitutional body (Articles 315–323) that conducts examinations for central services including IAS, IPS, and IFS.
Constitutional body (Article 280) constituted every 5 years to recommend devolution of taxes between Centre and states and grants-in-aid.
India's highest law officer, appointed by the President under Article 76. Has audience in all courts and advises the government on legal matters.
Second law officer of India; assists the Attorney General in representing the Union government before the Supreme Court.
National Institution for Transforming India — Policy think tank set up in 2015 replacing the Planning Commission; promotes cooperative federalism and long-term strategy.
Erstwhile central planning body of India that formulated Five-Year Plans; dissolved in 2014 and replaced by NITI Aayog.
National anti-corruption ombudsman established under the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013; investigates corruption complaints against public servants including the PM.
State-level anti-corruption ombudsman; investigates grievances and corruption allegations against state government officials.
Reserve Bank of India — India's central bank (est. 1935). Formulates monetary policy, regulates commercial banks, and manages the country's foreign exchange reserves.
Securities and Exchange Board of India — Statutory market regulator (1988/1992). Protects investor interests, regulates stock exchanges and listed companies.
Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India — Statutory body that regulates and develops the insurance industry; promotes policyholder interests.
Telecom Regulatory Authority of India — Statutory body regulating telecommunications services and tariffs; recommends spectrum allocation policy.
Competition Commission of India — Statutory body (Competition Act, 2002) that prevents anti-competitive practices and regulates mergers and acquisitions.
Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority — Statutory body that regulates the pension sector and administers the National Pension System (NPS).
National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development — Apex development bank providing credit and development support for agriculture and rural sectors.
Small Industries Development Bank of India — Principal financial institution for promotion, financing, and development of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs).
National Housing Bank — Apex regulatory body for housing finance companies; promotes a sound and healthy housing finance system in India.
Export-Import Bank of India — Premier export finance institution providing financing and promotion services for India's international trade and investment.
Monetary Policy Committee — RBI's statutory six-member committee that determines the policy repo rate; Governor has casting vote in case of tie.
Constitutional body (Article 279A) comprising Union and state Finance Ministers that makes recommendations on GST rates, exemptions, and model laws.
National Company Law Tribunal — Quasi-judicial body adjudicating company law matters including mergers, insolvency proceedings under IBC, and corporate disputes.
National Company Law Appellate Tribunal — Appellate body for NCLT orders; appeals from NCLAT lie to the Supreme Court.
Debt Recovery Tribunal — Quasi-judicial body for expeditious adjudication of debt recovery claims by banks and financial institutions.
Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade — Nodal department for FDI policy, IPR, Startup India, and the Make in India initiative.
Gross Domestic Product — Total monetary value of all goods and services produced within a country's borders in a period. Primary measure of economic size.
Gross Value Added — GDP minus taxes plus subsidies. Measures economic activity sector-wise (agriculture, industry, services) without tax distortions.
Gross National Product — GDP plus income earned by residents abroad minus income earned by foreigners domestically. Measures a nation's total income.
Rate at which RBI lends short-term money to commercial banks against government securities. Key monetary policy tool: raised to tighten, lowered to ease liquidity.
Rate at which RBI borrows from commercial banks. Used to absorb excess liquidity; always lower than the repo rate.
Cash Reserve Ratio — Minimum percentage of a bank's net demand and time liabilities kept with RBI as cash. Raising CRR reduces money supply.
Statutory Liquidity Ratio — Minimum percentage of a bank's deposits maintained in liquid assets (cash, gold, approved securities). Controls credit expansion.
Marginal Standing Facility — Emergency overnight borrowing window for banks from RBI at a rate above repo rate; borrowing against approved government securities.
Wholesale Price Index — Measures average price changes at the wholesale level (producer prices). Published by DPIIT; covers 697 commodities.
Consumer Price Index — Measures retail price changes faced by consumers. RBI's inflation target is CPI-based (4% ± 2%); published by MoSPI.
Index of Industrial Production — Composite indicator of short-term changes in mining, manufacturing, and electricity output. Published monthly by MoSPI.
Purchasing Managers' Index — Monthly survey-based economic indicator. Reading above 50 = expansion; below 50 = contraction. Key early indicator of economic health.
Foreign Direct Investment — Cross-border investment establishing lasting interest or management control in an enterprise. Regulated by DPIIT; key driver of economic growth.
Foreign Portfolio Investment — Investment by foreigners in financial assets (stocks, bonds) without gaining management control. More volatile than FDI.
Foreign Institutional Investor — Older term (now replaced by FPI) for foreign entities investing in Indian financial markets such as mutual funds and pension funds.
Non-Performing Asset — Loan where borrower has not paid interest or principal for over 90 days. High NPAs signal banking sector stress.
Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 — Regulates foreign exchange transactions; violations are civil offences. Replaced FERA; enforced by ED.
Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act, 2003 — Mandates the government to maintain fiscal discipline and cap the fiscal deficit.
Minimum Support Price — Government-guaranteed floor price at which FCI procures crops from farmers, protecting them from market price crashes.
Goods and Services Tax — Unified indirect tax (July 2017) replacing multiple Central and state taxes on supply of goods and services. Administered via GST Council.
Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 — Consolidates insolvency laws for companies and individuals; established NCLT as adjudicating authority; time-bound resolution.
Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 — Allows banks to auction defaulter properties without court approval.
Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 — Establishes state-level Real Estate Regulatory Authorities to protect home buyers and ensure project completion.
Current Account Deficit — Shortfall when a country's imports of goods, services, and transfers exceed its exports. Large CAD puts pressure on the rupee.
Balance of Payments — Systematic record of all economic transactions between Indian residents and the rest of the world, including current and capital accounts.
Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 — Key anti-money laundering legislation; empowers ED to investigate, attach, and confiscate proceeds of crime.
Annual document presented by the Chief Economic Adviser before the Union Budget; reviews economy's performance and provides analytical insights.
National Asset Reconstruction Company Limited — Government-backed 'bad bank' established 2021 to aggregate and resolve stressed assets (NPAs) of banks.
Central Bureau of Investigation — Premier investigation agency under Ministry of Personnel; handles corruption, economic offences, and special crimes. Supervised by CVC.
Enforcement Directorate — Multi-disciplinary agency under Ministry of Finance that enforces FEMA and PMLA; investigates money laundering and foreign exchange violations.
National Investigation Agency — Central counter-terrorism law enforcement agency (NIA Act, 2008); investigates terror financing, organised crime, and WMD-related offences.
Research and Analysis Wing — India's external intelligence agency (est. 1968); responsible for foreign intelligence gathering and counter-terrorism operations abroad.
Serious Fraud Investigation Office — Statutory body under Ministry of Corporate Affairs that investigates complex corporate fraud and financial crimes.
Public Interest Litigation — Legal action filed in Supreme Court (Article 32) or High Court (Article 226) for public benefit; available to any citizen regardless of personal standing.
Special Leave Petition — Appeal mechanism under Article 136; the Supreme Court may grant special leave to appeal any judgment or order of any court or tribunal.
Right to Information Act, 2005 — Enables citizens to request information from public authorities within 30 days; promotes transparency and government accountability.
Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 — Special law for protection of children below 18 from sexual abuse; creates gender-neutral offences and special courts.
Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967 — Primary counter-terrorism law; amended 2019 to allow designating individuals (not just organisations) as terrorists.
Armed Forces (Special Powers) Act, 1958 — Grants special powers to armed forces in 'disturbed areas'; controversial for immunity provisions. Currently in Nagaland, Manipur.
Citizenship Amendment Act, 2019 — Provides path to Indian citizenship for Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, Jain, Parsi, and Christian migrants from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan.
Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act, 2010 — Regulates acceptance and utilisation of foreign contributions by NGOs and individuals; requires annual filing.
National Register of Citizens — Register of Indian citizens; updated in Assam per Supreme Court directions to identify illegal immigrants from Bangladesh.
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 — Replaced the Indian Penal Code (IPC); modernised criminal law with provisions for organised crime, terrorism, and speedy justice.
Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 — Replaced CrPC; mandates video recording of searches, caps police custody, and integrates technology into trials.
Indian Penal Code, 1860 — The main criminal code of India defining offences and punishments; replaced in 2023 by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS).
Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 — Procedural law for criminal trials including investigation, bail, and sentencing; replaced in 2023 by BNSS.
Writ (Article 32/226) meaning 'produce the body' — requires a detained person to be brought before a judge; primary safeguard against illegal detention.
Writ commanding a public authority to perform a mandatory public duty it has failed to perform. Issued by Supreme Court (Article 32) or High Courts (Article 226).
Writ to quash an order of an inferior court or quasi-judicial body that has acted without jurisdiction or violated principles of natural justice.
Writ inquiring by what authority a person holds a public office; used to remove persons who have illegally usurped a public office.
Latin term meaning 'on its own motion' — courts (especially Supreme Court) taking up cases without a formal petition when a matter of public importance arises.
Tenth Schedule of the Constitution (1985) — disqualifies elected members who voluntarily give up party membership or vote against party whip without permission.
Process of redrawing boundaries of parliamentary and assembly constituencies based on latest census data; done by a Delimitation Commission.
None of the Above — Ballot option allowing voters to reject all candidates; introduced by Supreme Court in 2013. NOTA votes don't affect results.
Electronic Voting Machine — Battery-operated tamper-proof machine used in Indian elections; introduced in 1982, universally adopted by 2004.
Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail — Printer attached to EVM that shows voter a paper slip of their vote; allows independent verification of electronic count.
Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 — Protects SC/ST communities from discrimination and atrocities; provides for special courts.
Directive Principles of State Policy — Non-justiciable guidelines in Part IV (Articles 36–51) of the Constitution; guide the state in socio-economic policy-making.
Part III of the Constitution (Articles 12–35) — Justiciable rights guaranteeing equality, freedom, protection from exploitation, religion, culture, and constitutional remedies.
Part IVA of the Constitution (Article 51A) — 11 duties of Indian citizens added by 42nd Amendment (1976) and 86th Amendment (2002); not justiciable.
Bill under Article 110 dealing exclusively with taxation and consolidated fund matters; can only be introduced in Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha can only recommend changes.
Bill implementing budgetary proposals related to taxes; unlike Money Bill, it can be introduced in both Houses and Rajya Sabha can amend it.
Informal parliamentary device starting at noon; MPs raise matters of urgent public importance without prior notice; not mentioned in Constitution or Rules of Procedure.
First hour of parliamentary sitting devoted to questions by MPs to ministers; Starred Questions require oral answers, Unstarred require written answers.
Device to draw attention to a definite matter of urgent public importance; can be moved with Speaker's permission; leads to debate and adjournment of normal business.
Motion in Lok Sabha requiring PM and Council of Ministers to prove majority support; if passed, the government must resign (Article 75). Requires 50 MP signatures to admit.
Article 356 — Imposition of Central rule in a state when constitutional governance has broken down; proclaimed by President on Governor's report. Maximum 3 years.
Article 356 — Imposition of Central rule in a state when constitutional governance has broken down; proclaimed by President on Governor's report.
Introductory statement of the Constitution declaring India a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic committed to Justice, Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity.
Financial instrument (struck down by Supreme Court Feb 2024) that allowed anonymous donations to political parties through SBI-issued bonds.
Proposed reform to synchronise elections to Lok Sabha and all state assemblies to reduce election-related disruption, expenditure, and policy uncertainty.
Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005 — Guarantees 100 days of unskilled wage employment per year to rural households; largest employment scheme globally.
National Rural Employment Guarantee Act — The original name for the scheme renamed MGNREGA in 2009 in honour of Mahatma Gandhi.
Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi — Direct income support of ₹6,000/year in 3 instalments to eligible farmer families with cultivable land.
Pradhan Mantri Kisan Urja Suraksha evam Utthaan Mahabhiyan — Promotes solar energy in agriculture to reduce diesel use and provide stable income to farmers.
National health protection initiative with two components: PM-JAY (health insurance of ₹5 lakh/family) and Health and Wellness Centres (Ayushman Arogya Mandirs).
Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana — World's largest government health insurance scheme; provides ₹5 lakh/year cover for secondary and tertiary hospitalisation to 10 crore+ poor families.
Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana — Affordable housing scheme with urban (PMAY-U) and rural (PMAY-G) variants; aims to provide 'Housing for All'.
National sanitation mission (launched 2014) to achieve open defecation free India and improve solid waste management; Phase II focuses on ODF sustainability.
Urban development mission (2015) to develop 100 smart cities with sustainable infrastructure, IT-enabled services, and citizen-centric governance.
National initiative (2014) to transform India into a global manufacturing hub; promotes FDI, reduces regulatory burden, and builds manufacturing infrastructure.
National programme to build digital infrastructure, deliver services digitally, and promote digital literacy; flagship programme of the Modi government.
Flagship initiative (2016) to build an innovation ecosystem; offers 3-year tax holiday, self-certification compliance, and faster exit mechanisms for eligible startups.
Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana — Financial inclusion scheme providing universal access to banking, credit, insurance, and pensions; world's largest financial inclusion initiative.
Government pension scheme (2015) for unorganised sector workers below 40 years; guarantees pension of ₹1,000–₹5,000/month from age 60; regulated by PFRDA.
Pradhan Mantri Suraksha Bima Yojana — Accidental death and disability insurance at ₹20/year premium; provides ₹2 lakh cover.
Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Jyoti Bima Yojana — Life insurance scheme at ₹436/year; provides ₹2 lakh cover on death from any cause.
Ude Desh ka Aam Nagrik — Regional connectivity scheme connecting tier-2 and tier-3 cities; subsidises airfares to make flying affordable for common citizens.
Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana — Rural road connectivity programme providing all-weather road access to every unconnected habitation above population threshold.
National mission to train youth in market-relevant skills; implemented through PMKVY, National Skill Development Corporation, and sector skill councils.
Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana — Flagship skill training scheme; provides short-term certification courses and recognition of prior learning.
Production Linked Incentive — Scheme offering financial incentives on incremental production in 14 key sectors to attract investment and boost domestic manufacturing.
National Master Plan for multi-modal connectivity (2021) — integrated digital platform for infrastructure planning across 16 ministries to reduce logistics costs.
Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation — Urban mission to provide basic civic amenities (water supply, sewerage, green spaces) in 500 cities.
One Nation One Ration Card — System enabling PDS beneficiaries to access food rations from any fair price shop anywhere in India using their existing ration card.
PM Street Vendor's AtmaNirbhar Nidhi — Micro-credit scheme providing collateral-free working capital loans up to ₹50,000 to street vendors.
Jan Dhan–Aadhaar–Mobile — The three pillars enabling Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT); together they eliminate leakages in welfare delivery.
Direct Benefit Transfer — System transferring government subsidies directly to beneficiaries' Aadhaar-linked bank accounts, reducing intermediary leakages.
National Education Policy, 2020 — Comprehensive framework replacing the 1986 policy; emphasises foundational literacy, multidisciplinary education, and mother tongue instruction.
United Nations — International organisation (193 members) founded 1945; primary mandate is maintaining international peace, protecting human rights, and promoting sustainable development.
United Nations Security Council — 15-member body with primary responsibility for international peace and security; 5 permanent members (P5) hold veto power.
United Nations General Assembly — Main deliberative body where all 193 member states have equal vote; meets annually in New York.
United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization — UN agency promoting peace through education, science, culture, and communication. HQ: Paris.
United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund — UN programme providing humanitarian assistance to children and mothers worldwide; 190 country offices.
United Nations Development Programme — UN's global development network; publishes the annual Human Development Index (HDI) and supports SDG implementation.
United Nations Environment Programme — UN agency coordinating global environmental action and supporting countries in implementing sustainable environmental policies.
World Health Organization — UN specialized agency (est. 1948) directing and coordinating international public health; leads pandemic preparedness. HQ: Geneva.
World Trade Organization — International body (164 members) governing global trade rules; succeeds GATT (1995). Headquarters: Geneva.
International Monetary Fund — International organisation (190 members) promoting monetary cooperation, exchange rate stability, and balanced international trade. HQ: Washington DC.
International financial institution providing loans and grants for development projects in low and middle-income countries; includes IBRD and IDA.
International Bank for Reconstruction and Development — World Bank's main lending arm; provides loans to middle-income and creditworthy low-income countries.
International Development Association — World Bank institution providing concessional loans and grants to the 75 poorest countries; replenished every 3 years.
Asian Development Bank — Regional multilateral development bank (est. 1966); provides loans for infrastructure and development in Asia-Pacific. HQ: Manila.
New Development Bank — BRICS-founded multilateral development bank (est. 2014) for infrastructure and sustainable development in emerging economies. HQ: Shanghai.
Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank — Multilateral development bank (est. 2015, 57 founding members including India); finances infrastructure across Asia.
Shanghai Cooperation Organisation — Eurasian political, economic, and security alliance; India and Pakistan joined as full members in 2017. HQ: Beijing.
Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa — Grouping of major emerging economies; India hosted G20 in 2023; BRICS expanded to 9 members in 2024.
Group of Twenty — Forum of 19 countries and EU representing 85% of global GDP and 75% of trade; India successfully hosted the G20 Summit in New Delhi in 2023.
Group of Seven — Forum of 7 advanced economies (Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, UK, USA); India invited as Outreach partner to several recent summits.
Group of 77 — Largest intergovernmental organisation of developing nations at the UN (134 members); advocates developing-country interests in international forums.
Non-Aligned Movement — 120-member international forum for countries not aligned with major power blocs; founded 1961 at Belgrade. India was a founding member under Nehru.
South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation — Regional grouping of 8 South Asian nations (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bhutan, Maldives, Afghanistan). HQ: Kathmandu.
Association of Southeast Asian Nations — 10-member political and economic union; India participates through annual ASEAN-India Summits under the Act East Policy.
Quadrilateral Security Dialogue — Strategic forum of India, USA, Japan, and Australia; focuses on free and open Indo-Pacific, vaccines, climate, and critical technologies.
India, Israel, UAE, USA — Quadrilateral grouping (est. 2021) focused on joint investments in food security, clean energy, and infrastructure.
Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation — 7-member regional grouping connecting South and Southeast Asian nations around the Bay of Bengal.
Indian Ocean Rim Association — 23-member intergovernmental body promoting regional cooperation among Indian Ocean rim countries; India is a founding member.
Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership — World's largest free trade agreement (15 Asia-Pacific nations); India withdrew from negotiations in 2019 over concerns about Chinese imports.
International Criminal Police Organization — Intergovernmental organisation facilitating police cooperation across 195 member countries; issues Red Notices for international fugitives.
Financial Action Task Force — Intergovernmental body setting global standards to combat money laundering and terrorist financing; being on the 'grey list' restricts financial access.
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change — International treaty (1992, Rio) on combating climate change; 197 parties; holds annual COP (Conference of Parties).
Conference of Parties — Annual summit of UNFCCC member nations to assess progress on climate commitments; landmark COPs include COP21 (Paris, 2015) and COP26 (Glasgow, 2021).
Nationally Determined Contribution — Climate action plan submitted by each country under the Paris Agreement, detailing emission reduction targets and adaptation measures.
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change — UN scientific body (est. 1988) assessing climate science; periodic Assessment Reports are the gold standard for climate data.
Convention on Biological Diversity — International treaty (1992) on conservation of biodiversity, sustainable use of its components, and equitable sharing of genetic resources.
Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species — International agreement regulating trade in 38,000+ species of plants and animals; 183 member countries.
International wetlands treaty (1971, Ramsar, Iran) — India has 80 Ramsar sites (highest in Asia). Focuses on conservation and wise use of wetlands.
2015 climate accord under UNFCCC to limit global warming to 1.5–2°C above pre-industrial levels; legally binding for the 195 signatories.
1997 treaty extending UNFCCC; mandated emission cuts for developed countries in two commitment periods (2008–12, 2013–20); USA never ratified.
1987 international treaty phasing out ozone-depleting substances; most universally ratified environmental treaty (198 parties); credited with healing the ozone layer.
International Union for Conservation of Nature — Global authority on conservation; maintains the Red List of Threatened Species classifying organisms by extinction risk.
Global Environment Facility — Financial mechanism for international environmental conventions (UNFCCC, CBD, etc.); funds projects in biodiversity and climate.
UN-backed fund (est. COP16, 2010) to help developing countries mitigate and adapt to climate change; target of $100 billion/year from developed countries.
International treaty (1989) controlling transboundary movement of hazardous wastes and their disposal; India is a signatory.
Global treaty (2001) to eliminate or restrict persistent organic pollutants (POPs) that accumulate in living organisms and cause long-term health effects.
International treaty (2013) to protect human health from mercury pollution; named after Japan's Minamata disease caused by industrial mercury discharge.
Indian Space Research Organisation — India's national space agency (est. 1969). Landmark missions: Chandrayaan (Moon), Mangalyaan (Mars), Gaganyaan (crewed spaceflight).
Defence Research and Development Organisation — 50+ labs under Ministry of Defence developing technology for India's army, navy, and air force.
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research — India's largest civilian R&D organisation with 37 national laboratories; under DSIR. Areas: pharma, materials, aerospace.
Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Apex body coordinating agricultural research, education, and extension in India; operates 113 institutes.
Indian Council of Medical Research — Apex body for biomedical and health research under Ministry of Health; played key role in COVID-19 vaccine development.
Bhabha Atomic Research Centre — India's premier nuclear research facility (est. 1954) in Trombay; operates research reactors Apsara and Dhruva.
Department of Atomic Energy — Directly under the Prime Minister; oversees nuclear power generation (NPCIL), research (BARC), and nuclear materials (UCIL).
Unique Identification Authority of India — Statutory authority (Aadhaar Act, 2016) that issues the 12-digit Aadhaar biometric identity number to all residents.
12-digit unique biometric identity number issued by UIDAI to every Indian resident; enables e-KYC, Direct Benefit Transfer, and financial inclusion.
Hindustan Aeronautics Limited — Navratna PSU manufacturing military aircraft (Tejas, Dhruv helicopter, Su-30 under licence) and civil aviation components.
Chief of Defence Staff — India's highest-ranking military officer, heads Department of Military Affairs; advises government on tri-services integration and joint operations.
National Security Guard — Elite counter-terrorism force under MHA; 'Black Cats' trained for hostage rescue, VIP security, and IED disposal.
Central Reserve Police Force — India's largest Central Armed Police Force; assists states in internal security, counter-insurgency, and election duties.
Border Security Force — Central Armed Police Force guarding India's borders with Pakistan and Bangladesh; established 1965 after the Indo-Pakistani War.
Indo-Tibetan Border Police — Central Armed Police Force guarding India's 3,488 km border with China (Tibet) in high-altitude terrain.
National Cadet Corps — Tri-services youth organisation providing basic military training to school and college students; motto: 'Unity and Discipline'.
Agnipath Scheme (2022) — Short-term military recruitment model for 4-year tenure; recruits called Agniveers. 25% may be retained for regular service.
National Medical Commission — Statutory body (2020) that replaced the Medical Council of India; regulates medical education, sets standards, and licenses doctors.
University Grants Commission — Statutory body that coordinates and maintains standards of university education; provides grants to universities and colleges.
All India Council for Technical Education — Statutory body regulating technical education including engineering, management, pharmacy, and architecture programmes.
National Eligibility cum Entrance Test — Uniform entrance examination for MBBS, BDS, and AYUSH admissions across India; conducted by NTA.
National Testing Agency — Autonomous body (est. 2017) conducting national entrance examinations including JEE (Main), NEET, UGC-NET, and CUET.
National Council of Educational Research and Training — Autonomous body developing school curriculum, textbooks, and educational materials for central schools.
Indian Institute of Technology — Premier engineering and technology institutes of national importance; 23 IITs across India under the Institutes of Technology Act.
Indian Institute of Management — Premier management education institutes of national importance; 20 IIMs across India; graduates are in high demand globally.
National Highways Authority of India — Statutory body responsible for development, maintenance, and management of National Highways; under Ministry of Road Transport.
Airports Authority of India — Statutory body managing 137 airports including international airports; responsible for air navigation services across India.
Food Corporation of India — Statutory body under the National Food Security Act that procures, stores, and distributes foodgrains; implements MSP policy.
National Thermal Power Corporation — India's largest power generation company (Maharatna PSU); generates about one-fourth of India's total electricity capacity.
Oil and Natural Gas Corporation — India's largest oil and gas exploration and production company (Maharatna PSU); contributes to over 70% of domestic oil output.
Coal India Limited — World's largest coal-producing company (Maharatna PSU); accounts for ~80% of India's domestic coal production.
Bureau of Indian Standards — National standards body responsible for standardisation and quality certification; administers the ISI mark and gold hallmarking.
Sustainable Development Goals — 17 global goals adopted by the UN in 2015 as part of the 2030 Agenda; cover poverty, health, education, climate, and inequality.
Human Development Index — Composite statistic developed by UNDP measuring life expectancy, education, and per capita income; India ranked 134th in HDI 2024.
